title: About Me
text:
Hello, my name is Miles Durkin. I am a junior at the University of Nebraska–Lincoln, triple majoring in Broadcasting, Sports Media & Communication, and Advertising & Public Relations. I currently have an internship with Corn Crib, and this will be my second year in the program.
During my freshman year, I was a Husker Vision intern but made the switch to Corn Crib in my sophomore year. I’ve always loved watching intro videos at sporting events and dreamed of creating one myself. That passion sparked my love for videography and editing, which are now central to my work in social media and creative content. I’ve been doing what I love for around five years now.
Coming to Nebraska was the best decision of my life. It allowed me to be surrounded by like-minded individuals in a competitive environment where everyone pushes each other to improve and strive for greatness.
Outside of work and school, I love watching movies and listening to records. I’ve also recently discovered a new love for reading. I’ve always wanted to write and direct my own movie, so I’ve been studying film more and reading to help make that dream a reality.

title: The 2021 White Sox were the best team in baseball
text:
In the late 2010s, the Chicago White Sox were in the midst of a major rebuild. With top prospects rising through the minor leagues and young players gaining major league experience, expectations began to grow on the South Side.
A strong 2020 season, despite being shortened, ended in a playoff appearance and gave fans a glimpse of what the future could look like. Heading into 2021, the White Sox entered the season with one of the most promising young cores in baseball and expectations of becoming a true contender.
However, that vision was quickly tested. During spring training and throughout the season, key players were sidelined for significant periods. Eloy Jiménez missed most of the year with a torn pectoral muscle, Luis Robert missed roughly 80 games, and Yasmani Grandal also spent time on the injured list. As a result, the star-studded core rarely played together for an extended stretch.
This raises an important question: what would the 2021 White Sox have looked like if they were fully healthy?


Code
library(tidyverse)




Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggalt)
library(ggrepel)

PLAYERBATTING <- read_csv("data/2021_MLB_PLAYER_BATTING_DATA.csv") |>
  filter(WAR > 0)


Rows: 1696 Columns: 35
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr  (6): Player, Team, Lg, Pos, Awards, Player-additional
dbl (29): Rk, Age, WAR, G, PA, AB, R, H, 2B, 3B, HR, RBI, SB, CS, BB, SO, BA...

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.


Code
WS <- PLAYERBATTING |> filter(Team == "CHW")

LR <- WS |> filter(Player == "Luis Robert Jr.")

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=PLAYERBATTING, aes(x=G, y=WAR), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=WS, aes(x=G, y=WAR), color="navy") +
  geom_text_repel(data=WS, aes(x=G, y=WAR, label=Player)) + 
  geom_encircle(data=LR, aes(x=G, y=WAR), s_shape=.001, expand=.001, colour="navy") +
  annotate("rect", fill = "blue", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = 50, xmax = Inf,
        ymin = 3, ymax = Inf) +
  geom_text(aes(x=75, y=7.5, label="High War, Low Games"), size=4) +
  labs(title="The White Sox core was plaugued by injurys ", subtitle="The CWS had 4 players above the average War for a full season while playing a minimum of 18 less games", x="Games Played", y="Season War", caption = "Source: Baseball Reference | By Miles Durkin.") + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    )









One of the biggest is Luis Robert. In 2021, the average WAR for a full 162-game season is around 2.5. Robert put up a 3.7 WAR in just 68 games, which is monstrous production in that small of a sample. He was playing at a really high level when he was on the field, and if he stayed healthy, he probably adds a few more wins to the team pretty easily.
On top of that, there are a few other White Sox players who didn’t even play close to a full season but still ended up above that 2.5 WAR mark. That just shows how much talent was on this roster even when guys weren’t fully available.


Code
library(tidyverse)

WS_WIN_LOSS <- read.csv("data/WHITESOX_YEAR_RECORD.csv")


WS_WIN_LOSS_CLEAN <- WS_WIN_LOSS |>
  mutate(
    Win = ifelse(grepl("^W", W.L), 1, 0),
    Game = row_number(),
    Cum_Wins = cumsum(Win)
  )

ggplot() +
  geom_step(
    data = WS_WIN_LOSS_CLEAN,
    aes(x = Game, y = Cum_Wins),
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  
  annotate(
    "rect",
    xmin = 30, xmax = 110,
    ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
    fill = "blue", alpha = 0.08
  ) +
  
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 60,
    y = 85,
    label = "Luis Robert Injury Period"
  ) +
  
  labs(
    x = "Game Number", y = "Cumulative Wins", title = "Chicago White Sox 2021 Season Wins Progression", subtitle = "Step Chart of Wins with Key Injury Context", caption = "Source: Baseball Reference | By Miles Durkin."
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









Focusing again on Luis Robert, we can look at the exact stretch he missed and how the team performed without him. Robert was out roughly from game 31 to game 110, and during that stretch the White Sox did not really fall apart the way you might expect.
That raises a question. If Robert is such a high-impact player based on the numbers, why did the team not completely drop off without him?
The answer is that he absolutely is that kind of player, but it also shows something about the roster around him. The White Sox had enough depth, and they also got strong production from unexpected contributors and mid-season additions that helped keep the team afloat.
So instead of collapsing without him, the team was able to stay competitive, which says a lot about the overall roster make-up, not just the star power at the top.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggalt)
library(ggrepel)

WSROSTER <- read.csv("data/WS_FULL_SEASON_ROSTER.csv")

LAROSTER <- read.csv("data/LAD_SEASON_ROSTER.csv")

SFROSTER <- read.csv("data/SF_SEASON_ROSTER.csv")

library(tidyverse)

WS <- WSROSTER |>
  summarise(
    Name = "White Sox",
    Players = n_distinct(Player))

LAD <- LAROSTER |>
  summarise(
    Name = "Dodgers",
    Players = n_distinct(Player))

SF <- SFROSTER |>
  summarise(
    Name = "Giants",
    Players = n_distinct(Player))

BIGTHREE <- bind_rows(WS, LAD, SF)

ggplot() +
  geom_bar(
    data = BIGTHREE,
    aes(x = reorder(Name, Players), weight = Players, fill = Name)) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "White Sox" = "black",
    "Dodgers" = "steelblue",
    "Giants" = "orange")) +
  labs(
    title = "MLB Roster Usage Comparison (2021 Season)", subtitle = "Chicago White Sox vs Dodgers vs Giants: total unique players used", x = "Team", y = "Total Players Used", caption = "Source: Baseball Reference roster data | By Miles Durkin") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    title = element_text(color = "black", size = 15, face = "bold"),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white"),
    panel.border = element_rect(linetype = "dashed", fill = NA),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "grey"),
    axis.text = element_text(color = "black"),
  )









Looking at the total number of players each team used over the season, there is something interesting that stands out. The two best teams in baseball in 2021 were the San Francisco Giants and the Los Angeles Dodgers, two California powerhouses. Both of those teams actually used more total players over the season than the White Sox did.
So what does that tell us?
It really points to two things. First, a lot of teams across the league were dealing with injuries, depth issues, and roster turnover, not just the White Sox. Second, even with that kind of roster movement, the top teams were still able to outperform everyone else.
At the same time, it also shows how solid the White Sox roster was in certain stretches. Even with injuries, they still had strong contributions from depth players and unexpected breakout performances. Guys like Billy Hamilton, Brian Goodwin, and the early-season surge from Yermín Mercedes helped keep the team competitive.
We will never really know what the 2021 Chicago White Sox were fully capable of at full strength. The season ended up being more about injuries, missed time from key guys, and constant roster changes rather than the core group actually playing together.
When you look at everything together, the player value, how the season played out, and even how the roster compared to teams like the Dodgers and Giants, it is clear the White Sox had the talent to be one of the best teams in baseball. The problem was they just never really had that full group on the field at the same time.
So in the end, 2021 kind of turns into a “what if” season. It showed how good that core could have been, but also how quickly things can fall apart when injuries stack up. For fans and the organization, it is definitely a year that will be remembered as a missed opportunity during what was supposed to be the peak of the rebuild.
